1.synthesis:
Psalm 70 is a doublet of Ps 40:13–17 — the final five verses of Ps 40 appear here as a standalone emergency prayer. The main differences: Ps 70 uses Elohim where Ps 40 uses YHWH, and v. 5 differs slightly. The superscription 'to bring to remembrance (hazkîr)' — also in Ps 38 — probably refers to the azkārāh, a memorial portion of grain offering associated with prayer before God (Lev 2:2). Liturgically, Psalm 70 was likely used as a standalone petition of urgency — an abbreviated form of Ps 40 stripped to its essential plea, extracted for use in acute crisis.

The psalm's five verses are entirely petition and contrast. The primary petition — 'Make haste, O God, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me!' (v. 1) — is the Psalter's most urgent help-cry, the ḥûšāh (make haste!) repeated twice. The enemy petition is mirror-structured: 'let those who seek my life be put to shame and confusion; let those who desire my hurt be turned back' (v. 2) — shame, confusion, turned back, disgraced — contrasted with those who seek God: 'let all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; let those who love your salvation say evermore, “God is great!”' (v. 4). The closing verse maintains the individual voice: 'I am poor and needy; hasten to me, O God! You are my help and my deliverer; O LORD, do not delay!' (v. 5).
1.historical_context: The doublet relationship with Ps 40:13–17 demonstrates that individual psalms functioned as modular liturgical units that could be extracted and deployed independently. The Elohist substitution (Elohim for YHWH) reflects the Elohist Psalter's editorial practice. The mock-exclamation heʾāḥ heʾāḥ (aha! aha!, v. 3) — the gloating cry of enemies (Ps 35:21; Ps 40:15; Ezek 25:3) — is the verbal expression of schadenfreude that the psalmist asks God to convert to humiliation: the emotion they expressed becomes their experience. The letter to the Hebrews connects the urgent prayer to Jesus's own prayer in Gethsemane (Heb 5:7: 'He offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears').
1.literary_structure: The psalm's five verses are organized as: urgent petition (v. 1), enemy-reversal petition (vv. 2–3), seeker-blessing petition (v. 4), and renewed personal petition (v. 5). The contrast between those who 'seek my life' (šōʾaqê napšî) and those who 'seek you' (měbaqqešê kā) structures the middle of the psalm: the enemies and the seekers of God are defined by what they pursue. The urgency-frame (ḥûšāh in v. 1 and ʾal teʾaḥar 'do not delay' in v. 5) binds the psalm together as a time-pressured emergency petition.
1.theological_application: Psalm 70's brevity and urgency model prayer in acute crisis: when there is no time for extended petition, the essential elements are these five verses — identify the need, ask for reversal of enemy plans, ask for the blessing of all who seek God, and re-present the self's need with urgency. The 'poor and needy' self-identification (v. 5) is the correct posture for petition: not self-sufficient but utterly dependent on divine help and deliverance. The theological claim 'You are my help and my deliverer' (v. 5) is asserted in the midst of urgent petition as the ground of the cry: the urgency rests on the prior conviction about YHWH's identity.
1.christology: The urgency of Psalm 70's petition — 'make haste,' 'do not delay' — is the prayer voice of all who are in mortal extremity. The author of Hebrews connects this kind of urgent prayer to Jesus's Gethsemane petition (Heb 5:7): the one who offered 'prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death' was heard because of his reverent submission. The 'poor and needy' who identifies as such before God (v. 5) mirrors Jesus's own self-emptying: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Cor 8:9). Christ's urgency and poverty before the Father are the perfect enactment of what Psalm 70 models imperfectly in the psalmist.
